posts.title: Why GitHub Stars Need a Local-First Knowledge Base
posts.summary: Stars are not a feed. They are a personal library — and libraries only work when the data stays with you.
posts.author: Starcat

posts.title: Asking Questions of Your Starred Library
posts.summary: RAG is useful when the answers cite your Stars — not when they invent a confident tour of the public web.
posts.author: Starcat

posts.title: AI That Suggests, Never Auto-Writes
posts.summary: Starcat's AI rule is simple: recommend tags and summaries, but never write into your library without confirmation.
posts.author: Starcat

posts.title: Bring Your Own Keys — AI Without a Black Box Default
posts.summary: Starcat's AI path is BYOK-first: your provider, your keys, your rate limits — and your library stays local.
posts.author: Starcat

posts.title: From Flat Bookmarks to a Searchable Knowledge Base
posts.summary: A starred repo is a promise to your future self. Tags, notes, and local search are how you keep that promise.
posts.author: Starcat

posts.title: Release Tracking Without Turning Into Noise
posts.summary: Subscribe to the repositories that matter. Ignore the rest. Release alerts should shrink attention, not expand it.
posts.author: Starcat

posts.title: Why Starcat Is Native macOS — Not Electron
posts.summary: Native is not nostalgia. For a tool you live in every day, platform fit is a product feature.
posts.author: Starcat

posts.title: Stay on GitHub Pages Without Losing Your Library Context
posts.summary: The browser Companion keeps notes, tags, and Starcat context next to the repository you are already reading.
posts.author: Starcat

posts.title: A Tag System You Will Still Understand on Monday
posts.summary: Fancy taxonomies collapse. A small, selfish vocabulary survives — especially when AI only suggests and you confirm.
posts.author: Starcat
